You can easily walk to the town, but many like to ride bikes. There are  a few places with big corporate names: McDonald's, Taco Bell, Rite Aid, Albertson's, Vons, and CVS. If you forgot something for your trip, you can easily go get it down at the store. And if you're REALLY tired, the local pizza place can deliver pizza to your campsite. Too cool.

-The beaches are clean, wildlife is abundant, and you don't have the huge crowds of the LA beaches. There are beautiful tide pools, and a seal sanctuary just south/east of the bluffs.  We see dolphins playing just off the surf line every time we come. 

-Yes, the trains pass by. Yes, they are a bit noisy. But it's game with many families. The first one to yell "train!" is the winner. You get about a dozen or so per day. The noise is loudest in the Anacapa and Santa Cruz areas, since there are crossings there and the engineer MUST sound the horn by law. Once the last Amtrak train goes by around 9, you're done for the night. Once in awhile there is a freighter that goes by late at night. You deal with it. But one very cool aspect of this is that you can actually ride the train to the campground: Send the driver ahead with all the gear, and others can come later. The train platform is about a block from the campground.

OVerall, this is the best place to camp ever. People are friendly, and everyone is having a fun time.

How can you not love Carpinteria? The town has such an old school surf town vibe it's hard to leave and go back home....I've been enjoying the beach here for many years and have never had a bad experience. The beach rentals are cheap too - you get way more bang for your buck here than say, Newport Beach. 

It does get chilly early in the morning and later in the day, so make sure you bring a sweatshirt!
Just love the beach, campground and little town. Super charming. The best!

Love this beach.  Not many shells here but a really nice walk.  after entering the beach - go to the right and keep walking forever across the beach.  At low tide you can walk even farther.  Definitely a nice place to walk and if you do - you have most of the beach to yourself.

The beach is beautiful, but the park is overcrowded, park rangers rude and aloof,  never saw a clean bathroom and over run with ground squirrels.
 My friend who lives I'm Carp was hassled for dropping us off at the traffic circle for not paying for parking? She didn't park. 
My advise is to keep driving to El Capitan.

Been there my whole life,July 13,2015 was the absolute worse campsite, and surrounding area I have ever seen.  And we camp almost every month somewhere.  Carpinteria is a well liked place but the dry camping spots are not maintained anymore, trash, rodents by the the hundreds, bathrooms in Ana Cappa were so discussing.  Ants herding everywhere. Oh yes ants, rodents are natural, the state will change that policy when someone sues them for plagued and rabies, wait until someone gets deathly ill from them before they start get rid of that problem. It's not like a snake that comes out of their hole once in awhile, it literally is 50 squirrels in your camp site when you are there or not there. We were in a group site, Ana Cappa and the other dry camping sites are bad.  Oh if you need some water to clean up you table (rats, squirrels on it when you went to get something out of your closed tent) you need hiking boots to get to water and rubber boots to use the spigot it's so bad. If you love Carpinteria beach like we do, just stay in the rv area, with your tents, they have some spaces.

Go to Carpinteria if you want a retro visit to a beach (and town) reminiscent  of the 1950s. 

We love camping right on the beach or very very close.  We favor San Miguel campground for its quiet; Santa Cruz has more of a party atmosphere; Santa Rosa is full service for RVs.

We love the natural bluffs (bought and preserved by town residents), love the seal rookery, love the wetlands. A walking/biking trail is buffeted by fields of flowers and a golf driving range. You'll walk past an oil refinery, which also looks retro. Residents voted NOT to allow the company to expand, but the refinery tries to be a good neighbor. The rigs out to sea always look like Christmas decorations to me. (Okay, so I'm getting carried away here.) 

We love the quaint town (especially the Curious Cup children's bookstore, a rarity these days; the used book store run by Friends of the Carpinteria Library;  the Thursday farmers' market (incredible produce from nearby farms). 

We love the scents (eucalyptus, ocean, wild foliage) and sounds (ocean waves, frog serenade in the fresh water lagoon, laughing children, happy dogs and even the passing trains). Love the friendly people (residents, campers and, yes, the rangers, too.) 

To the earlier reviewer whose group was ticketed and warned: Just read and follow the printed state park rules. Yes, volunteer camp hosts check the dates on your windshield. Yes, children must wear helmets.  To the reviewer upset by after-hours gaiety:  I notice that your fellow campers quieted down when you asked, so why didn't you ask earlier? If you're shy about doing so, ask a volunteer park host to do it for you. That's part of their job, and they're stationed at each campground.

To those who complained about the animals, hey, you're in the out-of-doors and you're camping. Keep your egg cartons inside and bungee your coolers shut. Go to Best Western if you want to escape nature.
Like any state beach, it's kept clean and is beautiful. We didn't see dolphins, but if you're lucky you will!

The sites are stacked side by side. There are better sites in SoCal than this. Parking with a trailer is tight on busy days. More for small rvs since rows are now for turning in. The beach is cool and the tar mounds fun for kids.  But again, camping spot are very close to each other in Santa Rosa where we stayed sited 333. Everyone feels on top of one another. 
The only good spots are ocean front row!

The safest, friendliest, and the most calming place I've ever been to so far in my lifetime.  No, its not some type of tropical beach/Hawaiian paradise, but its pretty damn close to being perfect.  I used to live in Carp, I now live in the SF bay area and miss this beach so much.  There's always a spot for you to park your towel for the day, street parking is free, its family friendly, and if you forget to bring a cooler with food and drinks then be sure to eat at The Spot, which is just up the street.

We camp throughout the western states and have never experienced such an unpleasant and rigid ranger as Mike.  We made our reservations on line for midweek in February with the campground at most a quarter full.  When we arrived and discovered we were right next to another camper with most of the campground empty asked if we could move to another space and were admonished for even asking and suggested loosing one nights fee to re-reserve in the vacant space next to us.  Our neighboring campers were treated the same way when they tried to add days to their stay.  
There are plenty of people who need jobs, would serve the public better, with a smile and make an effort for the public who are paying his salary!
My favorite beach! Parking is free. Bathroom is clean. It is definitely a family friendly beach. They have restaurants around the beach that you can walk to. Overall, very satisfied!

I heard Carpinteria is a cute, sleepy little town so I veered off the freeway to make time to visit on my way home today.  You go through the little town to get to the state beach park and if you enter the park, you'll have to pay so if you're going to visit the beach, park outside on a side street and walk in.  The park has a huge campground and it looked pretty popular.  The beach is not very wide but is pretty long and the sand is fine and clean.  There's a volleyball court and a bunch of inns line the shore.  This would be a nice weekend getaway if you live in a crowded city...  It wasn't very crowded today but it's not the hottest day nor the weekend...  I saw one little snack shack but it was closed.  You may want to bring your own cooler with snacks and drinks though there are convenient stores near by.
I would definitely come back to lay out sometime.

I really liked it here but wasn't too Happy w our site..we went without reservations, so be sure to make a reservation, too pricey for the spot we had, but if you make it 6 months in advance you can get one on the beach, clean, nice hot showers.

Stayed at the RV camp grounds-- full hook ups. Take note, it is not like a resort (no pool, no hot water without a quarter in the showers, no lights in the paths). It is a state park-- go to the beach and ocean water, tide pools, and tie each other up in the seaweed. Rustic but so enjoyable by the fire and family. Some members of our party went to the Motel 6 for 70$ (they're not campers) but drove over every day for the "experience." Parking fee is 10$ each day. If you are a disabled person, it's 50% off the camp site reservation. Show your disabled person season pass. Parking fee still applied.

Such a clean and beautiful beach ! The camp ground is huge and a short walk to the beach. Definitely a great experience. David from maintenance was very kind and showed he cared for the state beach ! He definitely held a great conversation, he recommend a lot of spots with great views. I would definitely come back and recommend to anyone !

We don't camp, but we love this beach.  Bathrooms, lifeguards, family oriented.  Gently sloped beach perfect for a relaxing day.  The perfect time to go is between 4 and 7PM in the summer, when most of the crowds have gone home to make dinner, when you can enjoy the sun sparkling on the waves, and watch the dolphins and the pelicans, and the beach is at its restful best.  Heaven.  On a busy day, you need to stake out your space taking into consideration the tide (ask the lifeguard if it's going in or out) so you don't get overwhelmed by the waves as they make their way up the beach, or, if the tide is going out, you don't get stranded with others in front of you soaking up the view.  It's a great beach for walking, either down the coast to some unusual tar flows (kind of like lava flows, only tar) or up the coast, where we look at the beachfront homes and fantasize about which one we want.  :  )

I really cannot say enough about how much our family LOVES Carpinteria.  I started going here over 16 years ago when our friends used to have a rental they would get every year.  But then a few years back we went RV'ing with some friends and stayed in Carptinteria and LOVED it - absolutely LOVED it.  We loved it so much that we went and bought an RV, and since buying the RV we go to Carp at least once a month to just have a weekend getaway.

There is nothing better than being right on the beach.  The thing I love most about Carpinteria is that you don't need a car, you simply bring your bikes, scooters - and you are good to go.  It is just a short ride or walk into town - where there are some great little places to eat, there is a grocery store that you can stop by for extra bits and bobs...just LOVE IT!!!

This is a family tradition for us...we love it so much.  We have also just started our New Years tradition too...of spending New Years in Carp - it was a blast, having a bunch of us all there - PARTY fun!!!  RV hopping, LOL.

Great place, great people, great fun!!

Im just about 32 years old (Oct 19th) and Carpinteria was a beach my family took me and my older brother to, every Sunday of every summer vacation for several years when we were very little. The beach was beautiful and had a lot of character to it. It used to be very clean. 

About 3 years ago, I started an "annual BBQ trip" with very close friends. Every year for the last 3 years we all get together (about 12-15 of us) and head out to the beach for a very long day of sun and fun....yea I know it sounds cheesy. 

Anyway, upon re-visiting Carpinteria 3 years ago, my wife and I noticed that sadly, the beach is treated like a garbage can. From Gatorade and Budlight bottle caps, to cigarette butts and paper wrappings, can be found in the dirt....shoved in the sand, hidden. Its absolutely awful certain people would allow for this to happen and we really need to keep that beach clean and free from trash. Its nto very difficult to maintain the beach clean because all you need to do is prepare in advance and bring trash bags. 

And if you forget to bring trash bags, you can do one of two things: 1) you can walk down to the MANY trash cans available and dump your trash or 2) you can simply drive (or walk) 2 blocks towards Vons and pick up some trash bags. Problem solved. 

I could give this beach 5 stars, but because of the trash, and people who leave the trash (you know who you are) it gets a 4 star rating. 

Other than that and after we clean up the trash we find, we all have such a great time hanging out at this beautiful beach. I was there from 6am to 8pm. It is a first come first serve beach meaning you have to get there early if you want a good spot.

One of our absolute favorite campgrounds!

PRO's:  
YOU ARE ON THE BEACH!  (or steps away)
Showers are available!
Bathrooms are fairly clean.
Great for large group camping.
You're right next to the town and can walk anywhere in Carp.

CON's:  
It is very hard to get a campground here.
The railroad tracks are quite obnoxious.  Prepare yourself.
If you're not in a large group, the large groups can bother you.
60 years and still our family favorite.  Four generations of fun...whether camping or condo-ing, we love it.

There are four campgrounds, and they are not created equal. Anacapa and Santa Cruz are the best campgrounds. San Miguel and Santa Rosa are basically parking lots, and absolutely no space between sites. The train does go right by, but I didn't hear anything after 8. Others did hear a train about 2 a.m. but I heard nothing.

We had several families, and stayed one night in Santa Cruz and one night in Anacapa. We were lucky and got spots that were a decent size. There was room for the kids to move. Yes, a lot of spots are VERY close together, but it is still fun. The kids had a great time! The beach is beautiful! Plus, the bathrooms and showers are very clean, in both Santa Cruz and Anacapa. 

It's city camping, but it is fun and easy. You can have Rusty's deliver pizza to your campsite, or walk into town and get something. We all plan on going back next year!

Toilets free and $1.50 showers.
You must reserve in advance to stay overnight. Day use avail.
Camp site had too much debris from previous guests.
The ground is composed of very fine dirt that makes everything filthy instantly. The park needs to invest on mew ground-cover for guests. Ground bark and new soil would be nice. I know a piece of turf (big) would've helped somewhat but cant think of everything.
Park should clean up and rake sites on occasion.

This beach has always been one of my favorite places to visit when I'm in the area. The sand is soft, there is a nice amount of wave action, and in the summer they put out floating decks. The parking is free and there is a nearby campsite as well.

I rated it a 4 star instead of a 5 because of the tar issue (but don't worry, it doesn't happen often).

This place was a hit for us and I would definitely stay here again. We had a great time but next time we will stay further away from the dump station sites in the 300's much better. Nice place to take your pet and go for a walk close to all and walking distance to Rusty's pizza. We saw a lot of dolphins and seals.

I just spent an enjoyable three-night 4th of July weekend at this campground located on the shores of the Pacific and only an short hour+ north of Santa Clarita off the 101.

Camp Hosts were there to monitor the grounds and provide assistance and a ranger makes frequent trips through the sites.

Our campsite, along with others I surveyed, was free of debris and spacious enough to park an RV and SUV on the adjacent parking area. The restrooms were clean and well-monitored (never ran out of tp) and warm inside showers were available on a pay-for-use basis. A cold water shower with upper and lower spigots was also available outside the restrooms to rinse off the sand from your body or feet. I noticed also that some campers did their dishes using the large basin and hot water attached to the restroom's front wall.

An inviting well-kept beach beckoned for a stroll or just checking out the rocks and shells washed up with the tides. The surfers enjoyed the challenging waves and boogie boards were being used in large numbers. A repeat morning highlight was watching the dolphins and seals that passed by our line of vision in the campsite.

I highly recommend this easy-to-get-to "get-away" for RV campers (tent camping is also available in other areas) who want to take advantage of the cooler temps one can find oceanside.

This is a great park in a little town I loved it and had a great time.  First let me address the Rodent problem there was none.  I saw a few racoons which never bothered me and it should be expected that you are gonna see Racoons, Foxes, Rabbits anytime you go camping.  

Now the Sandy beach was beautiful and the trials and town were just clean as can be.  I would go back here for sure.

We LOVED camping here because we were RIGHT at the beach. Get a reservation for a beach spot, otherwise you are in one of three back rows and get to stare at us enjoying the beach. We had very jealous neighbors who cringed when I told them we had just booked the day before, and they said, "We have booked every year for 7 years 6 months in advance and you got this spot yesterday?" High rec for the spot, although the bathrooms (ICK AND ICK) I would not even go in there...that is why 4/5 stars.

My family and I have been camping at Carpinteria State Beach every summer since I was lil. Carpinteria State Beach is "The safest beach in the world" except for the occasional stingray bite. The beach is clean. The waves are kid friendly and free of rip tides. The floating deck passed the waves for those who dare. A school of dolphins reassure those who's greatest fear is sharks such as myself. Carpinteria State Beach campgrounds are smaller due to popularity except if you camp in Anacapa. The process of reserving a campsite at Carpinteria State Beach keeps a certain crowd away. The campers at Carpinteria State Beach are usually respectful but the raccoons and squirrels you need to worry about. No insect problems:) The restrooms were remodeled a few years ago. A Individual shower room is better than one restroom with shower stalls and prevent strangers from watching you shower through a puddle of water on the floor. The Rangers clean the restrooms daily. I prefer the Carpinteria State Beach restroom facilities over any other beach restroom facility, porta potty or no restroom when camping. This summer Pantene Pro V representatives handed out free generous sized samples of shampoos and conditioners campsite to campsite. The rangers provide 24 hour security, quarters for the showers, firewood sold for charity and children's activities at the visitor center. The visitor center provides an outdoor movie theatre. I remember the viewing of "Finding Nemo" when it was first released on video at the visitor center. A local pastor provides church service every Sunday at the visitor center. Carpinteria State Beach provides bike trails along the cliffs overlooking the ocean and continues on beside the rail road tracks. I find the train passing by in the middle of the night relaxing. Carpinteria State Beach campgrounds are walking distance from town for any forgotten needs. Camping at Capinteria State Beach is not roughing it! I plan to pass on the tradition of camping at Carpinteria State Beach every summer but I have yet to reserve my dream campsite of sand grounds number 31, 32 or 37 in Santa Cruz.

Carpinteria Salt Marsh Reserve was no nice and peaceful that we didn't want to leave the shores as we prepared to drive back to Las Vegas, Nevada, so we drove around slowly to distract ourselves, and found a walking trail north of Linden with the estuary. Carpinteria is so lucky to have the salt marsh reserve. We had a nice, enjoyable walk around the estuary before meeting with the Sunday weekend return traffic following the weekend in Southern California. Gorgeous trails for hiking.

Great great beach. It truly is safe.  Small warm water with gentle waves that break in shallow water.  The sand is perfect.  Parking is easy. YOU CAN DRINK ON THE BEACH.  Not in front of the day use parking, but on the beach in front of the campground, drinking is ok; so sit left of the kiosk road to have a few beers.  I love letting the kids play while I sip an Island brew IPA.  That makes for an epic beach day. I can have a few "mommy sodas" and not worry as much about the kids because I know Carp beach is safe. As in a big wave wont come and suck them out to sea.  The lifeguards are also helpful.

Great campground for all ages....very family friendly. Planning on coming back next year!!!! 

Pros: $6 firewood bundle sold at camp site, easy beach access, hear waves while you sleep in your tent, walking distance to small shops/restaurants, near Von's and Albertson's to get things you forgot back at home (about 1.5 - 2 miles away), dogs allowed, sites have large picnic bench table, fire pit, bathrooms are nearby (bring quarters....$.25 per 2 minutes), deep sink near restrooms to wash pots/pans. 

Cons: Train tracks 20 ft from our site ( freight train came cho-choin' by at 3AM!!!..but kids and baby slept right through it) , tent sites are way close to your neighbor,  but we quickly adjusted. 

Would recommend to anyone camping for the1st time or is new to camping. I came here with my family of 5 (3 kids under age of 5) and we had a great time our 1st  day. If you have kids, don't forget to bring their scooters, bikes, sand toys, and kites!

If you forget to bring simple camping necessities, stop by the nearby .99 discount store (cups, fork, spoons, tablecloth, etc..)

The beach is real nice here. Its sandy and not rocky. And the sunset is beautiful against the distant islands. The downside is that there was a lot of seaweed both in the water and on the sand. This was a problem cause it makes swimming uncomfortable and the seaweed on the beach draws and incredible amount of flies. I don't know what's wrong with these flies I guess their just laid back suffer flies but you can't shoo them away! It weird they just sit there, you have to actually flick them. We went on Friday in September and it was very lonely about four families. Also the town of carpintería is very nice.

The beach is beautiful, the sites are very close walking distance to the beach, and the walking/ bike paths are clean. I would recommend this beach as a great place to getaway for the day or for camping/ rving. This camp is very close to grocery stores in case of emergency needs. There are also lots of places to eat and shop nearby on Linden. The only part that turns me away from this place are the state park employees. They are fairly rude and lack knowledge of their workplace. I would still recommend the stay but try not to speak with any employee for too long. We were put in a spot with a full fire pit. We asked the kiosk workers if maintenance could empty the pit for us. They let us know if they had time they would empty and if not we would have to use the fire pit for the spot next to us. They also couldn't reassure us that the spot would not be taken. I was then told to get a shovel and a bag and do it myself. If they had said it in a kind tone I wouldn't have minded it so much. I don't mind doing a little extra work. Unfortunately they continued to be rude. At this point if you come here don't settle for their rude attitudes and laziness. Check your spot and make sure it's clean

Very nicely maintained camp ground.  We like the spaces that have no hook ups because they tend to be larger than those with hook ups.  Weather is really nice here as well.  We usually go in the fall for the avocado festival which is loads of fun.  There are restaurants and markets in walking distance as well.
Great beach for the family. Close to Santana Barbara. Quaint city. Great for a weekend getaway trip

Places change over the years... but for almost 20 years every summer my family would vacation here for a week and it was the big highlight for our summer. Our favorite spots were #41 and #40 but through the years we did try others when we did not get there soon enough for our first choice. The addition to hot water was nice for the showers and the remodel of the bathrooms was very good as well... basically this is a great campground, the events they do are also a lot of fun, the rangers are nice and keep the place in great shape. Love this campground!

Shells?? Yes please! Love this beach we found so many cool things during low tide (around 3:00). Kids had a blast! The bathrooms were clean parking was $10 but totally worth it. They have a nice picnic area complete with shade and a few BBQ grills. We will be back ;-)
So sorry this is so dog unfriendly.... ppl need to be responsible but restrictions are unfair to locals. Too many crazy homeless hanging out by Penny's cart.

I've been camping here for a long time.

Pros: Nice campground, nice beach, nice bike riding trails and beach town, great playground for the kids.

CONs :Tght spaces and a TON of gofer holes and ground squirrel that will eat everything if you leave it out.
 People do NOT watch their kids at all. Ya I have kids, but I teach my kids respect for others and don't allow them to walk thur people's camps or ride thru others camps as well. People should pay attention to what's going on and watch their kids. Kids just dash out in front of motorhomes and cars with out any supervision at all. parents just because you are camping you should still parent and watch your kids.
I went here a couple of months ago with mom I love the beach here peaceful and beautiful I will be going back again

This beach is as beautiful as any beach, anywhere. The sunsets are fantastic. The ocean is blue. There's always a nice mix of people there.  Lots of great facilities. Close to great eating places. No riptides. Lots of parking. And most importantly, it is located in one of the cutest little unspoiled beach towns anywhere.

The beach is beautiful, aside from the tar.  The campers are so nice and very helpful.  The biggest problem I had was with the park rangers.  They seemed to be bored-  I found them very rude.  We went with friends, we each had out own sites, but ate dinner together, and played games together after.   We were told by the park rangers that we had too many people at our site at once.
 (Seriously?? For dinner!??) 
Also we were told that we were being too loud.   We were playing pictionary!  
I just felt that the rangers were way too uptight.  The kids were having fun.  For the price we paid to be there, we should be able to have wholesome family fun!  
Other than that, I think it's a great place to camp.  The park rangers should occupy themselves with more serious issues.
Right near the ocean, not far from a cute li'l town (~1mi) with a variety of good restaurants, other amenities, and a neat li'l library.  Campsite is well-patrolled, better than average.

The whole place is one big mellow vibe. As one sales person in a surf shop once said, "I can't complain. I live where people come to vacation."

We've been coming to Carpinteria State Beach for years. It's our regular for getting away since it's close enough to the SF Bay Area and south enough for the water to start getting warm. We bodyboard and there's usually something that you can ride, whether it's stand-and-drop or paddle to catch a wave. In the summer, I've been able to wear a shortie and even just a vest. Most of the time, though, we're so cold-blooded that we need full suits since we're in the water for a while. Definitely more comfortable though than Santa Cruz area, closer to home.

It seems to be warmest in the summer and early fall. So, Labor Day get aways are warmer than Memorial Day. One thing that can be a funky is that sometimes there are small oil slicks and you'll get tar stuck on stuff out in the water. It's minor, but some people kind of freak out over it. Local hotels have little tar removal packets.

After spending the morning playing around in the water, call in a to-go order at The Spot. Pick it up and chow down on the beach. See the envy in the kids eyes who have their nutritious, but less fun whatevers.

Here's a video of dolphins, present a lot of the time, feeding pretty close to the shore:

http://www.flickr.com/photos/47079906@N00/3621106700/

An absolutely gorgeous beach at the end of Linden Ave. in Carpinteria. The state park also has a camground that looks equally georgous. 

The beach itself is clean, and according to signs the "safest beach in the world" (I'm not sure what criteria it's based on, but I do admit I felt quite safe while walking down the beach). The waves aren't too big, people were swimming without wetsuits (some had them, most didn't), dogs were loving life, sandcastles were being built, waves were being jumped. Oh yes, good times.

The houses... I would kill to live there in one of those houses. 

Couple things. We followed the signs to the state beach parking lot. Parking there is $8.00. Given we were there late in the day, we asked her if there were alternatives - the lady told us to drive through and find street parking. We found ample street parking (Friday around 5) - and of course, free. So check for street parking before entering the lot (and make sure you don't enter from the Linden side - tire spikes!!)

This seems like a great beach to try boogie boarding at. The waves are relatively small, and yes... quite safe-looking. It was a warm day, so wet suits shouldn't be needed if it's warm out. I regretted not bringing a swimsuit and boogie board, even so late in the day.

This is a must-see if you're in the area!

After 18 years in SB, I finally came to this famous beach.

It's utter perfection from a swimmer's perspective.  It is the sandiest beach in the area and the rocky part in the water is very short.

The water is glassy and there is no current or undertow, so it's like swimming in a pool with gentle swells.  

I am in love!  Parking is easy and free, even on a holiday weekend. 

Carp circa 2014 is not Carp circa 1996.  There are lots of food options and the liquor store by The Spot has good wine and beer, that is, 12/six-pack IPA and 10 buck Estancia Sauvignon  

I will swim here year-round and can't wait to pickup the used wetsuit I just took to Perfect Fit to remove the sleeves. It's essentially a triathlete spring suit except that the neck isn't cut out as in the triathlete suits the lifeguard described to me. 

Just 15 min in no traffic from Montecito, it's a nothing drive.

Apparently until last weekend, the city had wooden buoys so kids could dive or jump--which attests to its lake properties.  

Carp is smoke-free but saw plenty pf people smoking on street, though few vapers like myself. 

The second day I came, the water was a bit rougher but still placid enough when you get out toward the swimming buoys.

Love this beach great area great people great location you get to see dolphins sometimes. If you know some of the spot you can walk to the end of the beach where the tide falls and see some cool fish. I've been coming here for years and love it

We stayed here a few nights ago for one night and really enjoyed it. Being able to just walk to the beach is always a plus and everyone was so friendly. Carpinteria is a cute little town with a few good places to eat so that was fun to walking around at night time. They could definitely clean up the showers and bathrooms a little but for $50 a night to stay a step away from the beach I'm
Not complaining :) we will be back and with our pups next time.. Very dog friendly :)

Nice beach. Only come up here one time a year for the Avon Walk for Breast Cancer weekend. Every time the beach area was clean but a bit congested. That is probably because of the event but like I said, that is the only time I'm here.

The bathrooms are yucky. No doors, no paper. However, it is very close to shops and a lot of restaurants, and the train can drop you off right down the street. It would probably take a person less than 10 minutes to walk to the beach.

I think we came here at the wrong time. There was a lot of hicks roaming around the beach and the bathrooms were nasty. The experience was so horrible that it was hilarious. So, I guess that if you want a good, clean experience, don't come during mid-August.

I hadn't camped here since 2008, and was able to return in September. Some new things have been added and there has been other improvements. This has always been one of my favorite campgrounds. The town has some really great places to eat and some really nice shops. There is nearby bicycling for the hard core cyclist. My only beef and it's one that can't be helped is THE TAR!!!! Be forewarned that you will get tar on your feet/kids/dogs/beach stuff etc. Make sure you bring some kind of tar remover.

This review is only based on tent campsites.  We camped out there this past weekend and it's got to be one of the worst campsites we ever visited.  Can't believe they charge $35 per night!  It was literally a caged parking lot with narrow spaces between each site so there was not much room for a tent.  We would've been packed like sardines with other tent campers, but thank goodness they had plenty of cancellations due to the storm.  We had to move the bench around to make more room for the tent.   When we asked to use the fire pit by the ocean since it was so empty, they indicated that we are only allowed to use the pit on our site. :(  Very strict.  The railroad track was literally 20 feet away from our tent and the train ran into late hours, even around 11:30pm.  They kept tooting the horn everytime they passed by, which was extremely loud and annoying.  The bathroom was clean, however.  The beach was gorgeous.  We tried fishing, but the line kept getting caught on seaweed.  I don't think we'll be returning there, at least for camping.

Carpinteria.... I love to get away...


I've been staying here for years -personally and through work. My boss, who is also a family friend, is a camp ground host here during the summer. As a little girl, we had girl scout camp outs here, as a teen I spent summer day trips or weekends here, as a family we've camped out in their campground, and now as an adult, I take 50 teens here for a retreat every October. 

If you are interested in staying in the campground: 
Being that it is state run, the prices are comporable with that of other beaches. The sites are large, well maintained, and clean. The rangers are present, friendly, and reasonable. 

If you are visiting the beach: 
The beach is always clean, and rarely crowded. Great for families or groups of friends. Don't expect to get away with crazy shenannigans though - the beach is patrolled reguarly by rangers. Life guards are present which is nice for added safety. 

If you walk down the beach a little, you will find a tide pool. If you walk the other direction (towards the condos) you will find floating barges (usually out in the summer i believe). 

There are also several covered picnic areas for lunch or events. as well as well maintained bathrooms and showers.

Just came back from a two night stay. The campsite is definitely popular and more of a retreat area in comparison to other places where you'd feel like you're actually camping out in the wilderness. There were a lot of large families with children but it was not rowdy or too dirty. People mention that you will be close quarters to your neighbor- this is true. Bathrooms were decent and had hot water. Showers take quarters, but be weary that some stalls may just eat all of your quarters. Beach is a short walk away and it's clean. Saw a lot of people surfing or boogie boarding. We tried surf fishing with no luck... Then again we didn't try hard enough. Tidal pools aren't too great. Maybe saw about 7 sea anemones and that's all. Big cons are the trains that pass by every couple of hours starting at around 6:30 am and the fact that you're of walking distance to a small town with all the amenities you'd ever need. Definitely, not a "survivor" type of experience but good for some family fun

Tips:
The Carpinteria State Beach's FB has a photo album with a pic of all the camp sites
Someone mentioned in their post to bring baby oil/cooking oil to remove tar- good tip!
Squirrels and birds attack your site when you're gone so pack all foods in your car
Bring a sleeping mat. Sand is not comfy to sleep on
Campground host sells wood $6 a bunch, which is pretty cheap
Each individual campsite comes with a pit and a table (unless you rent the group areas)
If you're in a rut, check for cancelled reservations 48 hrs prior to the date you want to go
Bring a sun canopy!
Camarillo outlets are about 45 min away

Good site. Cool scenary. Could use more maintainance. Parking people suck. They will not help you out in any situation and give you only 15 minutes to pack up all your stuff and get out or sick the rangers on you. Be warned. Worse customer service and no respect. Something needs to be done about people like that who work there who cause crap like this for no reason. It's just controlling people trying to be an someone they're not. Just some sort of rent a cop bs. Go do that crap somewhere else. Just thinking about what Happened earlier gets me so irritated again. There is so much more to say but forget it.

Beautiful beach, have seen dolphins, seals and leopard sharks in a small cove past the city beach. Gets very crowded, so go early and by early I mean 830am-900am. Early mornings  there is a lot of seaweed by 1030am it has all cleared away and the water is clear.

The campgrounds are a short walk away! The beach is clean.  They have showers available. Just beware, there a gophers and squirrels everywhere. So be careful where you step cause you can fall into a gopher hole. Also the seagulls and squirrels are very daring. So don't leave food out. 

Yelp 100 Challenge (30 out of 100)

Carpinteria state beach, really nice beach off the highway 1. They have a pretty good set up here for those who are driving along the coast and need a place to stay for a night.  One night stay is $45 but you can only stay for the one night and then you cannot come back for 48 hours based on availability. It was one lane where 10-15 rigs can pull in along the fence of the actual camp grounds. Its far from glamorous but well worth the pay because we were only 50 yards from the ocean. When you first get there you do need to pay for the day pass which is $10. But then they take the $10 off the $45 nightly fee so you end up paying additional $35.  The staff at the check in gate were really nice, Doug was a volunteer and he was very helpful when we pulled in and gave us good information to help us for the rest of our trip. The surrounding area of the actual beach was awesome, there are plenty of places to go eat and walk. Several pubs to grab a drink, 
And several little stores to shop. On the beach they have ping-pong and volleyball with many tables for picnics.

Negative 
* no generator use after 8pm??? WTH!!  I feel if quiet hours start at 10pm then that's when generators should be turned off. The people running around making noise in the area were actually louder than our generator but we were asked to turn it off at 8 PM. Mind you, they did stop and wait until we turned it off.

10/16/11
What an Amazing time I had with my family!! spent the weekend there and the weather was amazing and the view was awesome...Really loved the hot showers...don't forget to bring the quarters...friendly neighbors parked/tented nearby...the area is safe and pretty clean...

Look forward to coming back again!

After living within Santa Barbara County for the last 7 years I never really ventured past State St. much. And then I found Carpinteria. A true "sleepy beach town" this place is the hidden gem of all beaches. On a hot day (in Carp usually 82-85 max) the beach is just sprinkled with people. The water is always gorgeous, you can find sand dollars and huge sea shells when the tide is low, and dogs are seemingly allowed to roam on the beach with a leash of course. The people are so friendly in this town and that is why I know have a condo here. The air is fresh and there is always something fun and new to do. I love walking my pug on Sunday on the beach, always making a pit stop at Carp's famous tree which you can't miss if you park in the parking lot at the end of Linden and look to your left.

Great place to camp near the beach. Beautiful town and nice people working at the campsite. 

The beach was clean and not at all crowded.
Carpinteria State Beach boasts to be the "world's safest beach".  It does not allow alcohol.  It's a great beach to come and chill with your family.

Oh how I love Carp State beach! My wife and I moved to Carpinteria about 7 months ago. We fell in love with the town the the get go. From the really nice people to the nice quaint down to the best beach in all of SB! Very clean, nice place sunset walks and overall wonderful experience everytime we go! The only reason why it didn't get 5 stars is since it's a state beach they don't allow dogs :( and being a dog lover it's sad that you can't enjoy this wonderful beach with your dogs. Overall great family beach and fun place to go!

Located within walking distance of downtown Carpinteria, the camping sites are small, typical for California State Campgrounds located on the Pacific Ocean.  Sites are about $50 a night which is not cheap.  Inland state park camping is much cheaper.

The beach is quite pleasant and street parking is convenient if you don't want to pay the day fee at the actual park.

Carpinteria is small, quaint town with a short drive to busy Santa Barbara, excellent for day trips.

Loved the campground with exception that you can't run your generator until 10:00 am and shut off is 8:00 pm. Makes it really difficult when you have kids and need your toaster or even to make yourself a cup of coffee. Some of us need that to get the morning going. Besides these negative aspects the campground is nice, close access to the beach and town.
This is a really nice each to go to and the water is pretty clear, definitely one of my favorite beaches to go to for the day

I know this is a campground and not the Ritz Carlton, but there are some basic necessities you need in a campground.  I'm OK with no room service!

My husband came here a few months ago with some friends and I was not able to make it.  So told him I wanted to go up again soon and we scored a site this past weekend.  

PROS
*  Carpinteria is a beautiful small town with a quaint main street with restaurants, shops and a convenient grocery store
*  Beautiful weather, beaches with amazing sunsets!!
*  We got a "beachfront" campsite - Site 18, which was only a short walk from the campsite - had toilets and showers for beach go-ers.
*  You could hear the waves at night from our site!!  Awesome
*  Showers were decently clean
*  Quick drive to Santa Barbara for shopping and wine tasting!!!
*  You can walk to the grocery store for any last minute items

CONS
*  When we checked in on Friday afternoon, there was a very grumpy older couple who helped us.  I use help resistantly.  Think they were the campground ambassadors, and not Park Rangers.  Either way, put a smile on your face!
*  Our site was right next to a big grass park which many people used for sports, running the dog or just hanging out.  Only bad this, is that all the campers would walk by our campsite to get there.  A bit too much foot traffic.  Would go for Site 20 or 21 next time.
*  Not enough restrooms, and they were disgustingly dirty - only 3 ladies and 3 mens for 27 campsites.  Put in a few more and make them all co-ed.
*  Unfortunately our neighbors next to us came home stumbling drunk at 130am, and preceded to talk very loudly outside their tent.  Guess you can't predict this, but they apologized in the AM.

Not sure if I will be running back here.

This is a beautiful beach and I love going here for a quick get out of LA getaway but this campground is awful for tent camping. I know the economy is in the tank and public campgrounds need to pay for the facility somehow but $50 for literally a 15x15 dirt patch is ridiculous. If you book tent camping you will get a parking space that you back into a dirt lot separated from the road by a chain link fence.

They advertise the site can hold 8 people but for the life of me I have no idea how they came to that conclusion. There is absolutely no way you can fit a tent for 8 or multiples of that on these lots. Even 2 spaces would be difficult to fit 8. I had booked 2 sites since our group was originally going to be 10 people. The day of our overnight we ended up with 5 but I kept the 2 spots since they were already paid for. We had 3 tents on 2 spots and we were bumping into each other left and right. Not only that but there is no demarkation between sites and you will be RIGHT next to other campers.

We did not have the same experience with noise that other campers mentioned. In fact we stayed up til midnight drinking and laughing at what we (drunkenly) considered to be quiet voices with no complaints from anyone. Rangers came by once at 10:15 and told us to lower our radio and keep a low tone. We did and everything seemed to be cool since we had no other complaints all night. Didn't hear the train either.

Sunday morning the place emptied out considerably. We were the only car on the lot at 12:30. Perhaps midweek camping is the way to go since you'll have tons of space and few other campers in your business. Camping sites beach side may be better since they're right on the sand but I'm not coming back here to try it. I will not come back to this campsite unless I have RV hookups. This is a terrible tent camping site and the big money seems to be invested in RV spaces that are much better kept with more space.
Love this place! Soft sand, wide beach for miles, lovely backdrop of foggy romantic mountains...

Spaces are tight, but if you're out playing all day, do ya really care? I heard people bagging on the showers, but I found them to have plenty of hot water and enough pressure to work those tired muscles. 

Bottom line, it's a bargain to be that close to the water. The camp hosts we met were awesome. And the location to town is real nice. Enjoy at day at the Carp! You won't be sorry!

Carpinteria is a simple easy going beach. The waves were huge. 

Simple . We walked the beach & yes it might not be the cleanest beach . Had tar all over my feet but any beach is heaven & zen. 

I do know the camp grounds are awesome. They have a lot of spots. The have local restrooms that's were clean. 

$6 for firewood. 

One volleyball court & ping pong table. 

Train goes right through there.

Intimate beach. Small.
This is a nice place to camp. Or moreso call it "glamping". The facilities are pretty nice. It is like a 2 minute walk to the beach and into the ocean. I didn't sleep overnight though.
Great place to camp. Clean bathrooms and hot water showers. Beautiful beach with lots to explore.

As an avid beach camper for many years I have to say, this place pretty cool. It's beautiful, easy to get to, kid friendly, the town is very quaint, and the campsites are pretty nice. 
I only gave it 3 stars because, the rangers are neo-nazis from hell who seem absolutely determined to totally harass you and ruin your vacation. I just returned from there yesterday. We arrived last Thursday, stayed the night, were having a nice Friday when this female ranger informed us that our trailer tongue was over the white line. LITERALLY 3 inches. I measured. We were on the end BEHIND a curb. Totally out of the way. The camp grounds were virtually empty. She made me hook it up (a pretty major hassle to those familiar with being ALL set up) and back it up the 3 inches even after I explained that the previous 45 other patrol cars seemed okay with it. Our fellow campers noticed this and started telling me that one of the rangers measured her dog's leash and found it 2 inches too long. Another threatened to give a fellow camper a ticket because her son who was 17 years old was doing ollies on his skateboard without a helmet. 
During last year's trip, my trailer tire was literally touching a fox tail and I had to move it for "parking on the grass" - Folks, this fox tail had grown over night and touched my tire. I was totally on the pavement. I was not "on" the fox tail. I offered to simply pluck the fox tail, but was told to move my trailer.
Now comes night time. Baby Hughey Mr. Ranger Sir (complete with state trooper hat) comes barging in to our campsite with his flashlight on. My dogs literally scared the crap out of this dude who came to tell us that we were talking too loud. There were 3 people camping near us (again, the place was virtually empty) and ALL 3 of our families were talking over the fire. Baby Hughey says - "calm your dogs down and use your library voices" HEY DOUCHE! You are the one who scared my dogs. They were sleeping before you barged in here like a super cop with an important mission with your little flashlight blazing and all. It seems with all of the budget cuts and State Park closures these people could try to make you feel welcome. I understand rules. I've been camping the California Coast my whole life, but these rangers really suck. All of that said, I'd still recommend going.
Nestled between the beach and downtown Carp, this campground is great for an overnight. They also have a bike/hike area.

On my last road trip back home (SD at the time) I was tired of driving, and I kept missing my exits. I wanted get some fresh air near the beach, and a chance to stretch my legs. 

It looked like I had passed all the beaches and parks, but I tried my luck and got off on Ballard. To my surprise that exit dumped me off at Carpinteria Bluffs Open Space.

It's a rest area slash beach slash doggy park. The parking lot was pretty full. It seems to be a popular place. I got off, stretched my legs and walked down a red tainted trail that led down about 700 yards to some willow trees and the beach.

I didn't make it all the way down, but it's a great place to stretch from a long trip or let the dogs out (not your bunions) and run around to do their business.

I saw a lot of families, and older folk with their dogs. The area looks pretty safe and clean.

My fiance and I had a great time. The campsite is smaller than it looks on the map, so it's a little cramped if you are next to a large group but also close to the facilities... 

One thing I want to point out is that the park ranger (Liz) we spoke with was amazing. We came up to cycle around Santa Barbara and not only did she lend us a map of bike routes but took the time to explain her favorites to us. We had an excellent time. I didn't notice any park rangers patrolling the campsites after dark... The other ranger seemed incredibly friendly and helpful and left us alone. 

Also the town is really cute and falling asleep to the waves crashing was spectacular. I highly recommend booking a reservation in advance as it seems to fill up quickly.

This place is pretty great! What a wonderful beach! Snorkeling, paddle boarding, kayaking, surfing, and all kinds of fun beach activities to be had. There is a small day use area which provides shade. 

The campsites are pretty small. It's car camping for sure. The bathrooms are clean and there are even showers. Yes, there are squirrels around, but you're camping! What do you expect!

If you're considering this campsite just book it. You won't be disappointed.

First time camping here, LAST time camping here!!  Spots are right on top of each other.  Dirt and weeds everywhere. First time I've ever camped and gotten ants in the trailer. Camp hosts are one training class away from being full fledged gestapo!  They advertise and post pictures like the campground is on the beach but almost every spot is far from the beach and a huge mountain of sand blocks any view of the ocean you might think your going to get....   #disappointed

OVERRATED! Very cramped, most sites are literally 6 feet apart, NO privacy. Even off season mid-week was just packed which would be ok if the sites weren't so dang tiny. Barely enough room for chairs & a tent. Bathrooms/showers were filthy. Beach was OK, nothing special. Didn't find anything interesting in town either....

This is our Family beach.  Always our Go To.  It is clean, safe, and a short walk across the sand to the water.  We always seem to find parking right away when we arrive.  Sorry if I keep the parking spot a secret. The kids love playing the shallows and we feel very safe that the drop off is far out from a long decline.  We have found an abundant amount of things to do here.  Pop up a canopy and chairs.  Have a picnic lunch.  Launch a kite and tie it to the canopy.  It'll stay in flight he entire time without faltering.  We build sand castles.  We dig for Pismo Clams in the shallows(note: they have to be measured 4.25 inches across and have a Salt Water Fishing License.  I saw DFW check someone who was clamming).  We dug up sand crabs and used them for bait to fish for Perch.  We once found a big Mole Crab that freaked me out.  There are a lot of places up the street to find some eats.  Local places are the best.

I heart this beach with all of my being. I walk on it just about everyday and it is always pleasing. Almost zero trash, great swimming and surfing, tons of wildlife, and cool rocks that have tar seeping out of them, makes this beach a fan favorite. *Don't forget to bring baby oil to help get the tar off your toes!

Very beautiful beach. The kids always have a great time. Lots of beautiful people. The only down side of this beach is when you lay or sit in the sand, you smell like an oil can afterwards.   I guess to the offshore drilling. I also had a laughable movement with my children when I stepped in a glob of oil. Besides that, its a beautiful beach.
Nice beach, the only downside is the tar that gets on the bottom of your feet and clothes while walking in the sand.

I went here after visiting the zoo to have a picnic on the beach. We had a great time. We would have loved to camp here but it was crowded .

Check out my Road Trip to Santa http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=18oVdfa2nyU&feature=relmfuBarbara video. Some Clips include the beach

Second time I came here, & I loved both visit.
Just bring a jacket and some shoes..
** Recent visit was in May, was super COLD obviously there haven't been any Sunshine in Socal. **
this trip was awesome, water was freezing cold, but I finally walked the trail they have to go see the Sea Lions. Pretty Coooool!

* but leaving in the morning with a trailer is a hassle and everyone trying to get out at the same time.

First stop from L.A.X bringing our friends from Colorado. This is a beach that's beautiful and fun. It's not for surfing (waves aren't very big, but great for little ones) but for relaxing, chillin', and camping with family and friends. What's great about this beach is the location. It's close enough to drive, but once there seems worlds away from the hustle and bustle of life. The little town is cute and quaint. Starting in April they close down the street every Thursday for a farmers market. They show a movie for the kids at the park next to the campgrounds. The views, sunrises, and sunsets are breathtaking. We arrived in mid-morning and walked along the beach; the tide was very low and we were able to look at the tide pools. We were looking at everything then, as if on que, dolphins started to frolic right in front of us about 20 feet. It was a perfect time for our first stop for our friends. I've lived in California all my life and I make it a point to camp, fish and stay here. Great for a family getaway, recommend for anyone.

This is the nicest beach I have been to in California. Aside from being pretty much just a GREAT beach, the area is also fairly low-traffic. Not a ton of people, an overabundance of houses and shops crammed along the beach-front and plenty of free parking. AND, this is also supposed to be the "SAFEST BEACH IN THE WORLD", so there aren't a ton of critters, ocean monsters, undertoes or hobos around trying to kill you.... Always a good plus in my book. ;-)

Summer in winter and the place was empty with prime beach spots available.

Nothing like the sound of waves and wind lulling you to sleep...plus the train.

This is a perfect spot to take a break from life as you know it. 

Beautiful park. When it's not packed.

So this place is supposedly famous for being the "safest beach in the world"...that is probably because there isn't anything here except seaweed, dead crabs, dirty looking small waves and a vista of the oil rigs. 

They do charge a State Park entrance fee, so if you do decide to pay this place a visit, do yourself a favor: find street parking and hoof it in.

Two stars for the nice facilities: restrooms and picnic areas.

Not impressed as there are far better beaches up and down the central coast.

Undoubtedly (& self-proclaimed) "the world's safest beach." Just watch out for the tar; some oil and a paper towel will take it right off.

Other than that, venture from one side to the other and you'll see a sea anemone colony, tons of kids playing, volleyball courts, and lots of happy people. Venture away from the beach and you'll find some awesome grub, great antique stores, and lovely views. Enjoy a movie right off the beach, in the park, occasionally too! Excellent town to vacation with the family.

A gorgeous beach with soft, moist sand.  The water looks so much cleaner than anything down south near us, but it's still too cold for my delicate sensibilities.

We spend the afternoon sitting in beach chairs staring at the ocean, digging our feet into the sand.  A very relaxing place to enjoy life.

Just went to Carpinteria for the day last weekend for the parks 80th anniversary celebration.  So much to see in the park I spent more time walking around looking at things.  It was a minus tide, first time I've been at Carp when the tide was so low and WOW!  What amazing tidepools!  See http://www.caopenspace.org/carpinteria.html for a few tidepool pics from last Sat (2/18/12).  I walked the beach, past Jelly Bowl and Tar Pit Park to the Carpinteria Seal Sanctuary, lots of Harbor Seals and their pups.  This is about the only place publically accessible in Southern California to see a seal rookery.  A new creek mouth restoration was just completed too, included a lot of pretty native plants (which in the spring when things bloom should look even nicer).  I've put this one on my "must camp" list, I like the spots practically right on the sand next to the creek mouth, I bet that would be really peaceful at night.

It is pretty awesome that you can walk all around town from the camping site.  Plus the beach is so very calming.  I've seen dolphins once in awhile.  The shells are sometimes abundant in winter, but if the tides are rough then its a bit iffy.  There is a really decent bluff walk past the campsite (to the south or is it to the west?).  Also, near the campsite (well okay within 3-4 blocks) there is a very nice marsh walk.  The smells of the marsh are so refreshing, I know it sounds weird but just trust me.  I didn't know about the state park charging for showers that is kinda lame, but there are a lot of RV'R's at sometimes of the year (well alot of the time) so I guess they have all they need.

Great beach but glad we saw it before deciding to go camping here. The campsites don't look very nice at all really- just a bunch of tents in a cage next to sand. Dressing room and bathrooms are super clean.

The beach is clean and free parking is around the corner at Linden Ave. Beach lot costs $10.. we came at around 4 pm so it wasn't very hard to find something at all. The water was pretty warm, even though it felt freezing when we first went in. I couldn't believe how clear it was.. I grew up in NJ with brown Atlantic Ocean water. Lifeguards are around which is nice. We walked to the area with tide pools and it was pretty cool. Saw the following: 1 sea anemone, 1 dead jellyfish (kids were freaking out about this and continuously warning me not to go in the ocean), 1 sand dollar, tons of dead crabs. Good time. I did somehow get tar from the tarpit thing on myself. I didn't touch it.. but it was all over my foot and on my dress a little too! Maybe from going in the water?

I go here once a year with my boyfriends family for their annual camping trip. I started going with them about 3 years ago and have a great time every year. The camp sites are close to the beach which is nice for romantic strolls at night. They also have a large grass field where we love to play soccer and fly kites. The site is also walking distance to Carpinteria's shopping centers. All in all, this is a great place to come to for a family get away. Cant wait to go again.

Been here before and it was always the nicest beach for kids. Unfortunately today, the tar was out of control. A little tar is expected now and again however when my little grandson said my feet look like a Dalmatian, gotta wonder what's going on.

Nonetheless, the camp ground was full of tents and the water was full of summer camp swimmers. Water is turned off at the showers and the parking lot spigot now.

So, bring your own water to rinse off and don't forget the tar remover, oh and of course, your sun screen.

Let me start by saying this review is biased Die to the beautiful sun that shone upon me during my visit. Really though, I don't know how many can honestly give God's creation of ocean and sand a negative review...wooooosah. I decided to relax here for a bit on a Sunday afternoon and was blessed to find no crowd. 

It's located at the end a street full of neat shops and yummy restaurants. A friend and I decided to take a post-breakfast walk down the street and voila...beautiful beach. There's also a park nearby if you have kiddos. 

It's adjacent to many vacation rentals that can be rented weekly or monthly. Down the street is also a Vons and Rite Aid. Good area for visitors to stay or check out. If you're looking for the beach, some grub, and a bed, Lindon Ave. may be the street for you.  The sand is soft too. Enjoy!

Four words: Best beach camping EVER!

We have been camping at Carp every 4th of July for the past 8 years.  It is a beloved family tradition that we have continued to to carry on even after our move to the bay area.  It's THAT worth the drive!

I know people find the spaces small, but it's beach camping...with hookups! And if you go on a holiday, you have to expect crowds.  But those things have not stopped us from going year after year.  We camp with family and friends, so all those others are just more people at the party! 

It's kid friendly, meaning I can sit in my chair while my kids bike the loop around the campground alone.  For as crowded as it is, we have always found the other campers to be respectful of quiet hours.

So, to sum it up, it's safe, fun, close to the beach and is a great vacation spot.

I loved it here! We were in town for my son's high school football game & had 4 hours to kill between dropping him off & kick off so we went to the beach! We got here around 10:30am, we found parking right away, walked along the trail to a nice picnic table (they have lots) & we just relaxed & watched the waves. My daughter looked for shells while our other teenage boys tossed a football on the sand. We noticed the camping facilities nearby & think we may go back. If anything I'd consider going back just for a day trip because its only 3 hours away & it's way less crowded than Huntington or Newport & lets face it, with OC & LA traffic it pretty much takes 2 hours to get there & find parking, just finding parking is awful then walking to the beach is a whole other nightmare not to mention how crowded our OC beaches get! I'd rather drive farther for less hassle! Very clean restrooms as well!
Nice beach.  Easy access.  Spacious.  Carpinteria is a nice, small town with adequate services.  Good for a weekend get-away.

Watching the sun set from the bluffs is something one shouldn't take for granted. Not only is is the scenery stunning, but the story behind Carpinteria Bluffs is amazing. A very dedicated and visionary group of Carp citizens raised nearly $3.55 million dollars to purchase the land from a developer and place it into public hands. They raised the money in a single year--just goes to show how much the Carp community loves and appreciates their environment. 

I certainly appreciate being able to run along the perfectly carved towpath which parallels the coast. Tonight I saw nearly 40 seals out on the beach and had perfect views of the Channel Islands to the west and the mountains to the east. It doesn't get better than this. 

For more information about the bluffs check out http://www.carpinteriabluffs.org

Large public beach adjacent to a popular campground.   Neither the beach parking nor the campground rates are cheap, but the location is terrific.  

We took visiting family here last Saturday afternoon,  along with a good chunk of Southern California!  It was so hot inland that it made sense that many people fled to the beach.  Still, we got a parking spot, grabbed our gear, and staked out a spot.

The younger nephews were off obsessing on Pokémon Go, while the rest of us hung out.  I walked down the beach for a while, which was quite clean.  Nice to watch families having fun in the Pacific to cool off.

Seemed to be a good place for body surfing. The waves broke close to shore.

Even with all the folks there, it was still relaxing to watch the waves, and feel the ocean breeze on my face.

Came here for camping since it's been awhile that I've camped by the beach. I was really exited until I arrived and things weren't what a beach camping experience should be... The beach was beautiful though. Clean, beautiful wether during low or high tide.

Reasons for a three star (camping): the campground is really dusty. Once the wind picks up it just blows the dust everywhere. Then there's the noise, a big problem. You're relaxing at one point then BAM! The train starts to pass by... Another noise contributor would be the neighbors. Not their fault though, they try to squeeze in too much campers, in so little space. Campground staff are mostly rude too. We asked a simple question and they glared before finally one of them answered the question. And the amount they charge is ridiculous for such a small camp space.

Overall, would recommend to just go to the beach, because it is beautiful. As far as camping, wouldn't really recommend it. But if you're with people you love to be around with it really doesn't matter, go.

Carpinteria State Beach is one of the most amazing camp grounds to be able to sit by the fire and look at the ocean and see the oil rigs all lit up for the holidays period to know that the park rangers or doing your job to make sure everybody's safe. Thank you very much Charles.
Spots are a little close but have ample room. Fire pit was a bit rusted out but still worked. Great that they have full hook ups. Price is good. Close to the beach. We will be back.

If you're looking to get away from it all, then this is not the place for you. If however you're looking to get right up along one of the nicest beaches along the the American Riviera, then you will have a hard time doing better than Carpinteria State Beach.

The spaces are tight, and unless you're here during off-season, it will be packed like sardines. That's OK though, cause there's plenty to see and do in the neighborhood. Carp itself is a wonderful old-school Cali beach town, with shops and food all within easy walking distance.

The waves provide a soothing soundtrack, sunsets are to die for, and evenings will not disappoint. Picnic benches, fire pits, sand, and surf. What more can you ask for?

Book ahead -- this place fills up well ahead of time.

Some good memories made here and more to come.  It's mostly about the company you have around you, but the nice setting doesn't hurt either.

One of the nicest beaches for camping, relaxing, and enjoying the beautiful California weather.  I am not sure if there is a dog friendly area of the beach, but let's just say my pup had a good time once on a portion of the 12 mile stretch of sand.

Note: the authorities are fairly strict with the campground rules and they do patrol around the site.  Don't drive too fast as there usually is a lot of kids/dogs running around or on bikes and scooters.  I was not the driver, but I have been in a car that got pulled over before... park ranger kind of hassled us but eventually we got off with a warning.

Fun fact from the website: "The Spanish named the area Carpinteria because the Chumash tribe, which lived in the area, had a large seagoing canoe-building enterprise, or "carpentry shop" there, because of naturally-occurring surface tar which was used to seal the boats."

Let me tell you a story about a place called carpinteria , it sounds magical but is no Narnia and there is no damn magical wardrobe, if this was EurekA this would not have happened... My manhood touched one of their dirty toilets in the spider infested lavatorys I proceeded to try and cleanse it by dousing my manliness in hand sanitizer at which point I flopped in pain like a fish out of water, also there is oil in the ocean water and the creepy neighbors all male stared at my iron bumper never going back Santa Barbara is pure fecal matter also as I am writing this it quiet time as the train ten feet away blares by loud as a raging thunderstorm on viagra and a man on his lawn mower powered hipster mobility helper scooted by very annoyingly. This has left me bewildered and confused I thought this was suppose to be a campground but I can watch the tv through the apartment complex across the street if I chose to but I choose not to because it's the news and news sucks phallics..
Whatta shitty place,parking lot nazis calling them selves park ranger 70 $ for a spot in the parking lot size of a small bedroom. Place sucks. Never again.

We haven't camped here for 13 years.  We just purchased a new trailer so we thought this would be a great close to home break in trip.  
The weather was a little on the cool side (damn cold at night) but beautiful.   This was a great pre-Christmas vacation with a chance to relax during the  chaos of all the mall shoppers.

Nice Beach.  Clean.  Plenty of parking even for RV parking.  The bathrooms are clean and convenient depending on where you setup on the beach.  Very crowded, though, even on a weekday.  I couldn't imagine the weekend.

Loved it here. Spent a solid 4 hours just hanging out there enjoying the weather. The sand was white and nice, not filled with trash and crap. They also don't mind you digging a big ass hole there. We decided to dig one using someones' random shovel left by where we were sitting and then we buried one of our stripper friends in it. I guess burying a stripper on the beach just seemed appropriate. I would give this beach 5 stars but there were no good waves for body surfing. The water is pretty clear too, I saw a fish swimming by my feet at one point. They have platforms you can swim out too as well. Parking was easy and plenty of it in the neighborhoods surrounding which was awesome, didn't have to pay just to park. If I lived around here I would hit this place up often.
Stopped by to scout the park for a future RV trip I am planning. Place is right on the beach and very clean. Good amenities and perfect for tenting as well!

Always nice coming to Carpinteria State beach. This is a great place to camp not because the campsites are awesome which they are small and can be cramped,  but because of the location.  You can ride your bikes into town which is only a couple of blocks away for a ice cream, lunch or dinner.Even the super market is only blocks away.   Your not dealing with a busy PCH next to the campground like other beach locations which makes is relatively safe for riding with the family. The ocean in Carpinteria is generally calm with smaller waves.   
On the other hand,  there are the trains. When the Amtrak comes through, it's not so bad (horn),  but the freight trains can be extremely loud!  
All and all, great place close to home (LA).

This place is AWESOME!
It's my all time favorite place to go camping with my husband and dog. The state beach is clean, camp hostesses are friendly & helpful and the campers who camp there are so friendly. 
The campsite has the beach 50 ft from my trailer spot. There is also a handful of hiking trails and Downtown Carpinteria is a 15 minute walk away, full of cute shops, restaurants and an amazing candy shop. :) 
I really enjoyed taking my dog on a hike near the natural tar pits. The trails are lined with mulch and very clean. I plan to come back again for many years to come.

A very accessible beach. Just drive down to the end of Linden Ave., park and you are there. (Don't go to the end of the parallel street to the south which is the entrance for the camp grounds.) Restrooms with covered picnic area within walking distance. Shower next to the parking lot. Nothing else but clean, uncrowded beach in either direction. When you get hungry, there are several restaurants just up Linden.

We didn't find allot of shells but we did find a few sand dollars and smooth rocks.

Really enjoyed this park. We were driving a 33' RV and needed plenty of room for 11 people to spread out. Our site was large, we had two picnic tables, a fire ring, and plenty of room for a large tent. We reserved site "Plover" and was located very well for restrooms, trash, and beach. The beach was wonderful - big wide open, sandy, with plenty of swimming. Slightly to the south you can explore tide pools (very cool) and further to the south you can see a harbor seal sanctuary. The ranger made his rounds often but never bothered anyone. In fact it was very comforting knowing that someone was keeping order. I know other reviews mention it but I too have to complain about the showers and the train - yes I said train. There is a train that feels like its about to come right through your tent at 1:30 am, 5:00 am and again at 9:00 am. The showers did take quarters (not tokens) but they were not in good working order. The restrooms were fine. 

Although the train and showers were my two complaints, they do not out weigh the positives noted earlier. I will definitely camp here again but next time I'll bring ear plugs.

Very disappointing weekend camping at Carpinteria State Beach.  Live just down the freeway in the western outskirts of the San Fernando Valley and thought spending some time at the beach for a camp weekend would be nice.
We were mistaken, sad state of affairs with our state parks systems.  $50 a night for a site plus $10 for an extra car that had to park in the visitors parking lot even though there was nobody next to us for the next six sites.The sites were so pathically small it was ridiculous, not to mention not very well maintained.  It did afforded sewer, electric and water hook ups.  The beach front spots were all taken, but they would not have been any better as far as size, so we were given the "next best spot" #85.  
I have never seen so many camp hosts in a camp park. No less than 10, and I agree with the other post concerning the park rangers.  Just rubbed me the wrong way the entire weekend. So needless to say we won't be staying here again.  There are other areas near or on the beach where you get better value for your dollar.  
The beach itself was ok, though not very clean.  They need to drag the sand for cig butts and other trash. Sycamore Canyon has better tide pools.  Sadly we won't be coming back.

What:
 - Beach
 - Hiking
 - Tidepools
 - Harbor Seals Preserve
 - Seals
 - View of Chevron Oil Pier
 - View of Rincon Island
 - Platform Henry Heliports
 - Birds

More Info:
 - Free admission
 - Free parking at the junction of Linden Ave and Sandy Land Rd
 - Restrooms available
 - Popular for camping

Carpinteria Bluffs Trail

Length: ~5 miles; Elevation: 300 feet; Time: 3 hours; Difficulty: Easy

On a warm Saturday morning in November, we drove a little over 10 miles south to Santa Barbara to go to the Carpinteria State Beach. We parked the car for free at the junction of Linden Ave and Sandy Land Rd and started walking south on the beach sands. The place appears to be a popular camping destination; we saw a good number RVs in the parking lots.

Carpinteria Beach - http://www.yelp.com/biz_photos/carpinteria-state-beach-carpinteria?select=l2a-ajgRDitCxodgfiHI5A#l2a-ajgRDitCxodgfiHI5A

In a few minutes, we noticed the Carpinteria Creek to the left creating a lagoon at its mouth. We continued walking and reached the Visitor Center where the Carpinteria Bluffs Trail starts off. All along, we could see the Chevron Oil Pier. The Rincon Island could also be seen to the far south.

Carpinteria Creek forming a lagoon - http://www.yelp.com/biz_photos/carpinteria-state-beach-carpinteria?select=l2a-ajgRDitCxodgfiHI5A#pdB4Dyr2f2K9kRX_sWXnjA

The Tar Pit Park, which is the entrance to the Chevron Oil Pier is a private property, hence closed to the public. A little past the Chevron Oil Pier is a detour leading to the Harbor Seal Preserve. The Harbor Seal Preserve is at an elevation and between December and May, it is a common sight to see a slew of seals on the beach. As we were here in the third week of November, I believe we were lucky to see a couple of seals just off the shore. Even otherwise, this is an excellent vantage point to see the Chevron Pier, Rincon Island and the vast ocean. The Platform Henry Heliports, which are 4 nautical miles away from the Carpinteria Beach could also be seen from the trail.

View of Chevron Oil Pier from Harbor Seal Preserve - http://www.yelp.com/biz_photos/carpinteria-state-beach-carpinteria?select=l2a-ajgRDitCxodgfiHI5A#B8nras1EYvRML1aIDEJpIw

We continued walking on the Carpinteria Bluffs Trail, until the trail splits into two; the one to the left is along the coastal bluff and the one to the right leads one to the beach. We took the path to the beach. The unique rock formations here resemble the skeleton of a whale. There were several places where we noticed oil spills on the beach sands and on the rocks nearby.

Unique Rock Formations - http://www.yelp.com/biz_photos/carpinteria-state-beach-carpinteria?select=l2a-ajgRDitCxodgfiHI5A#AGUdp9mQj_jenl19kRHyXg

It was a beautiful sight seeing the pelicans hovering over the ocean, diving deep into the water to catch fish and other tiny birds walking on the beach sands. We then reached a spot where there was an artificially constructed structure and there was a trail adjacent to it. There were ropes tied at every corner and we used them to climb on the steep bluff and reached the trail. The Amtrak railroads run very close to that trail. By then, we were enervated and returned to the parking lot.

Tiny birds on the beach - http://www.yelp.com/biz_photos/carpinteria-state-beach-carpinteria?select=l2a-ajgRDitCxodgfiHI5A#dQGHCseN1-2N0pYiQuw1mg

Tips:
