I said I was drinking and don't think i could drive my family (4 year old and 8 year old) pulling this trailer home. He said how long do you need I said I need to sleep on it. He said that's too long and he'd have me towed. So he then implies that I can have "a couple hours" to leave the premises. So we packed up and ate with our friends and left.

Look at photo fir the rest of the review

Probably one of my favorite beaches along the Central Coast. I love the vibe of this beach community. I've been camping there, the Avocado Festival, the restaurants, breweries and wine bars. All are good! It has a very layback beach vibe and moves at a slow pace. I definitely recommend grabbing the Amtrak Surfliner from the LA area, just layback and check out the scenery on the way to the beach. It drops you off in downtown and across the street from a brewery. Win!

Wonderful, relaxing and enjoyable beach.  This California beach not only allows one to chill out under the sun and lounge, but if you like to fish, you can.  If you want to picnic, you can.  If you want to camp, you can.  This palm-laden beach location offers an incredible view of the sunset.  It is not far from the urban areas, so accessibility was quick and easy.  Also local eateries and shops are almost a stone's throw away.

Booked our trip in Feb and screwed up the dates.  Luckily we were still able to find a spot when we showed up on Mar 16th - the last one!  The staff were super helpful and friendly.  Our only complaint would be that the spots are very small, making it tough to back our 21' trailer in and of course then being so close to your camp neighbors.  Dogs are allowed in the campground but not on the beach, so we ended up driving a ways north to Arroyo Burro so that our pup could splash in the water.  Other campers were really nice and there's just an overall good vibe in the park.  I felt very comfortable and safe.  Hubby lost the key to our truck while out and about walking the dog.  Another camper found it and was able to get it back to us after quizzing us to make sure it was ours.  Super thoughtful and much appreciated.  We'll be back again, but will hopefully be able to get a bigger oceanfront site.

I was excited to come up to Carpinteria for the first time. We do a lot of beach camping but had never stayed there. Though I had heard good things about the campground, all-in-all I was disappointed. Campsites were super small, campers had no etiquette and  it was not just crowded but overcrowded.  Too bad because I loved the town of Carpinteria. Great shops and restaurants very nearby. 
If you want a good beach camping experience near there go to El Capitan or Refugio, no shops or restaurants near them but the campsites are nicer.

This is one of the best urban ocean camping locations I have ever camped. It is practically right on the beach (our spot was a short walk across a parking lot) and so nice to fall asleep to the waves crashing through the night. It has a great open green grass area for play and a short walk to the playground. It is also a block from the downtown restaurants, shops and breweries. Now that is some excellent camping arrangements!! Weather was really nice most of the days we were there but pretty windy one night. TIP: Make sure everything is secure before you go to sleep here!! 

The bathrooms were okay but 4 of the 6 showers in our area (Anacapa Loop) were out of order. That seems like a lot and makes me think someone just didn't want to clean as many. But, the toilet stalls were clean every day. The water spigots provided some good drinking water and the hosts had firewood. 

We walked to the tide pools and found a bounty of shells and sea stars. There was quite a bit of tar, which is the biggest drawback (at Santa Barbara County beaches). 

PRO TIP for TAR: A paper towel and some oil (vegetable, olive, baby or coconut) will rub that stuff right off your feet. It is magic. I like coconut myself and keep a little jar in the car and camp trailer for this purpose

I grew up in Carpinteria and have never camped here. I decided to book a spot with reserve america and just try it out. I had space M155. Last weekend I took a walk over there to see how our space looked and to figure out how we would lay it out. OMG the space is SO SMALL... We have a 4 person tent and if we put it on the campsite it self, the fire would touch it and we would have to put the table and chairs on the parking space right in front because there is NO ROOM...

We canceled our reservation so we can look to see if any corner spots were open. Those looks to be the size of two spaces put together. One of the corner units was on a slop though so we might just look for another section towards Linden ave to have a camp site, when ever those come available LOL

I'm still going to camp in my beautiful town but just those sites, they need to redo them.
It's hard to find a bad place where sand meets Pacific. The waves are cold but friendly. It's a good spot for long beach walks.

My wife and I enjoyed this place that we spend two weekends here the past six months. 
During covid times, staying at home and be tough, so my we ended up booking reservations for this place to get away and have a nice change in scenery. Only being about 1.5 - 2 hours from Los Angeles, it's the perfect spot to get away, without the "get away" drive times.
We stayed at Anacampa campgrounds which focuses on tent camping, but RVs are here as well. The campground includes a fire ring and a picnic table, and about a 20 x 20 foot space (I am bad with distance, so maybe 25x25').
Your stay also includes communal toilets and showers. Showers have hot water, but you have to pay to use (a quarter for 4 min?). There is also a few communal sinks for dishwashing.
Other campers have been pretty courteous about quiet hours and didn't have any issues. 
The only caveat right now is that the communal restrooms that have the showers, sink, and toilets are under improvements right now, so the Park has porta-potties to use instead. Sounds gross, but dam, these really are the cleanest porta-potties. And they are upkept everyday in the morning. Those that still prefer to use toilets, there is a very clean beach toilet across the parking lot (about a 2m walk), or use the restroom facilities in the neighboring Santa Cruz campgrounds.

Would highly recommend. Campgrounds are well maintained. We will be back here for sure!

If you can get a camp site, you're a lucky one!!! The Bots are taking over and making it totally impossible to get a premium site reservation for RV's on Beach Row, site #358 to #380! Try to make a reservation 6 month out, when the sites open up on Reserve California at 8AM on their website and the clock hits "0" and you instantly click on your chosen site, it is already gone! Very frustrating! 
Per some articles that I have read, they say there are these bot sites that will reserve you a site, but you have to pay a huge premium per night to them in order to get one! This fee is over and above the cost of the the site on Reserve California! 
These sites should be outlawed they are making it totally impossible for the regular joe to reserve any premium site at Carpinteria and other State parks!
It was tough before they changed their reservation process, now it is impossible!

Great video review here - https://youtu.be/Anzi4H3Qxpk

Carpinteria State Beach Campground is such an incredible place to kick back and unwind.  The spaces are tight, but we managed to fit our 35 motorhome in a sweet little spot (348) right across from the beach.  Loved this spot because 347 right next to it was being used for maintenance's golf cart so we didn't feel quite as crammed.  

The sites up against the sand are prime, but shorter and about 25 feet of concrete in length, but allows for maybe 4-6 feet of overhang on the sand.  So really these spots are designed for 30 foot or less.  Basically, from your back wheel forward you'll need to measure and if it's over 25 feet, you should look for another spot in the campground.  

There are 4 campground areas, some with no hook ups for tent camping, some with electric and water only, and then the Santa Rosa Loop with full hook-ups including sewer which is where we opt to stay.  It is a State Campground so yes, you have to wake up 6 months in advance of your stay and play the whole internet scramble on reservecalifornia.gov, but our group of campers always seems to pull off the week and spots with some success.  They do have showers, restrooms, and overflow parking available as well. 

The town of Carpinteria is only a short bike ride away and offers quaint small town charm.  Good food and shops are worth taking an afternoon to explore.  For some reason each year the guys from our group have made it a ritual to take an afternoon to eat at the local Thai restaurant and grab few pints at the local brewery.  There's also a trip on bikes with the kiddos to hit the local candy shop.  

There is a marsh and some tidepools that are worth checking out too.  Eagle rays, leopard sharks, harbor seals, star fish, sea anemones, and all sort of ocean life can be seen throughout the area.   

The Spanish named this area Carpinteria because the Chumash tribe that originally occupied the land had created a large seagoing canoe enterprise, or "carpentry" shop here.  They used the naturally occurring surface tar to waterproof the canoes.  Well this surface tar still exists and is something we've had to deal with a bit. The kids will come back from playing in the surf and get spots of tar on their feet and swimsuits.  Not a big deal, but make sure to bring some baby oil to help make it easier to get this tar off of skin.  It'll really helps cut down on the mess.

No dogs allowed on the beach past 10 or 15 feet from beach front campgrounds, but bring your pup because there are plenty of places to still walk and take your buddy on adventures.  

There are campfire pits at most spots, at least in the Santa Rosa Loop so bring firewood to enjoy at night.  

If you're looking to rent an RV there is only one outfit that is permitted to deliver an RV to the park: 101 RV Rental Concessions and you can reach them at 805-210-7391.

I've camped at Carpenteria state beach and spent days enjoying the gentle surf and warm sand at the beach itself. It's one of my favorite places in California.

The beach is a long sandy arc dotted with tide pools. Dried kelp washed up from the huge beds offshore decorate the sand. A myriad of water birds keep company with families playing on the shore. Sometimes you can spot seals fishing in the dark, cold water not far from the surf. The weather is often foggy, and quite a bit cooler than inland. I should mention the off shore oil derricks and naturally occurring tar that can stick to you feet. Even with those annoyances, to me it's beautiful and idyllic. 

The campground is basically a 1920's parking lot. People and campers are packed together like sardines on the asphalt. The rangers are the grumpiest I've ever met. (But they keep the noise down, and with so many campers packed so closely together I think that's wise.) A train runs right past the campground intermittently all day and night. (Don't worry it's gated off.) Due to its popularity the campground is always in use and as a result probably doesn't look it's best, but none if that matters because you get to park your trailer or pitch your tent right next to the beach itself. Once I snagged a spot on the sand. It really was camping heaven. 
Parking is available on the street in town or you can pay to park all day in the large state park parking lot. There is a playground nearby in Linden street, and lots of places to eat a short walk away.

It's my favorite beach in California!

Our family has been camping at Carpinteria State Beach  for over 40 years. The location is great, beach is super safe, and the experience for kids in particular is a plus. They have also made accommodations for the disabled.

However: 

1. The reservation system is a mess and allows people to hold onto spaces until a day prior to arrival. In a system where spaces are limited statewide this is unfair and makes planning and access to camp spaces restrictive. People are allowed to book too many spaces at a time under the same  account. Also the old reserve California system while "no prize" and itself unfair to use, at least had an option to set your account for cancellation alerts so you could go online and book open spots. I complained about this to the state over a year ago and all I got was, a, we'll look into this.

2. The restrooms while improved, are poorly maintained and cleaned only once a day. There are not enough toilets and showers (both indoor and out). 

3.  There are also not enough locations for cleaning and washing dishes in the dry camping areas. Good luck trying to find a non ant infested sink. Also The water spots are located on or in people's camp spaces which causes you to violate others privacy to get water, or flood their camping space. The drains are usually clogged, and are marked as a hazard by an orange cone.

4. As with volunteers...The campground host have ttoo much discretion, authority, and are not cycled out fast enough. They're  supposed to make sure restrooms are stocked and if necessary do a clean up...that doesn't happen! As hosts They often have excess people themselves, and excess vehicles on the campground. They give their own friends and families "free reign" to parking, and use of the facilities,. The rest of us "payers" are forced to fight for the left over spaces. 

Hosts have excess parking in both  the camp and spots adjacent to the restrooms. Some former camp spaces (old Santa Rosa space 90) and a few others at the ends of the camp rows were converted (sacrificed)  into extra campground host parking. This is too much!... as they have space at their own spots and then access to  excess campground host "only" overflow areas. 

The hosts are also allowed to rope  off excess camping and living space for themselves. Even so much that they add their own "fencing" so u know where their space is and where you are not welcome. They pick and choose who to regulate the rules upon and are less than friendly and helpful. I recently spent a week being smothered by a large family of 20+ who had two spaces but were allowed to put all of their tents into an extra 3rd space that they weren't assigned to. The host spoke to them and did  nothing about the head count (8 per spot) and their "bullying" their way onto our camp space....and... their regular walking  through our camp to access their own. 

5. We now pay different rates for different days of the week. Yet we still have to pay for hot water. At one bathroom, two of the three outdoor Santa Cruz showers, didn't even work. That's tough when u consider when the state completely and brilliantly shut off the outdoor showers. 

The state also closed prime spaces for the aforementioned camp hosts, and then re- lined the  spots without considering the location of the hookups isn relation to power and sewer ports on RV and trailers. So now you have to run lines under and across your vehicle and camps space to hook up. 

6. Security is an issue and the hosts have no night time responsibility to walk the park and make sure thieves or others are loitering the camp grounds at night. Local PD have to commit to this. Yes the budget was cut on the staffing of full time rangers ( who cover multiple campgrounds) ..but cmon can't the hosts at least take turns walking the camp ground after hours so we don't become vacation victims. They are staying for free...aren't they?!?!?

Just a few thoughts...

This past week was my first time camping at Carpinteria beach with my boyfriends family. I have been camping before but nothing like this trip. We were here for a week. Arrived on Tuesday morning and left the next Tuesday afternoon. The beach itself is really , really nice. Really pretty and for the most part the weather is pretty decent. 

A lot of the people here (if not all) have trailer homes and tents for camping usage. We got a spot right on the beach. We sat up our tents on the beach and went to sleep and woke up with the sound of the waves every day. For the most part, the beach didn't seem crowded at all , which was probably the reason that everyone had their own spot on the beach (on the campground sites). 

There is are bathrooms and showers near by. The showers take quarters for hott water, which run about 3-4 minutes long. Everyone is pretty good about taking quick showers. If you want to avoid the wait, take a shower early in the morning or take a shower late at nigh around 9pm or so. The bathrooms for the most part are pretty clean and well stocked with toilet paper. They also have some wall outlets for hair dyers and the like. 

The campground are also close to downtown Carpinteria, which is a good walk to where you wanna go. There are also grocery stores near by to make yummy dinners each night. Over all a very nice place to camp, gorgeous at night, can see the stars. They also sell firewood on the campground, bundle for $4 or $6 - not bad. 

Not the Ritz Carlton, but after all this is camping - so this is as good as it will get!

Been coming to Carpinteria State Beach since I was 14 years old so figure 42 years ..brought my family of five and two of their friends got a great spot #401 at San Miguel, close to the beach about 150 feet and bathroom access close by & away from Folks for this pandemic.. Corner Spot so it worked out perfect highly recommend Carpinteria & little town is nostalgic but up-to-date folks here are very nice and welcoming. We had dinner for the last night camping at Rincon Brewery best place highly recommend.

Traveling with a pop up camper, family of four roadtripping from Ohio to California over a three week span. Being from the Midwest and camping a lot through there and the South, Gulf Coast and areas in between, I must tell you this has not been a pleasant experience for any of my family. I have never met so many unpleasant, unkind people in my life. Our kids are 15 and 10 and they can't believe how poorly they have been treated in these camp grounds. I pray it's been "Murphy's law" or a fluke but we can say with certainty, we will never camp in California again. We are currently at Carpinteria Beach State Park and it has been the WORST experience of my life. Camping? It's packed in like Walmart on Black Friday. The facilities are disgusting and the camp volunteers are ridiculous. We have felt singled out more than a few times because of out of state plates. We have received TWO citations on our vehicle while there over two days. One for being 2 inches over the parking line (we drove a Ford Expedition EL and we were given a walmart parking space to for our truck and camper in for 3 days. We had been in the campground 20 minutes enough time to unhitch, pop up the camper, try to adjust it to fit and sit down to figure out where to park. He didn't even attempt to say "welcome to Carpinteria, we are happy to have you camp here but we will need you to make sure your vehicle is inside the line" etc. He sat and watched us unhitch and then sped down to ticket us.  The other citation was for parking in the 15 minute Lot outside the pay showers to take a 5 minute shower. Wash, dry, dress and run to the car. We made it as the officer was writing the citation and he was a real jerk to us. The volunteer elderly woman on a powertrip golf cart gave us a verbal and written warning for our very small compact generac generator running at 10am because her watch didn't say 10 yet, one minute to go. She intentionally stalked out site by turninng around in front of it several times to take pictures and write her note. How about some welcome hospitality first?  Seriously people. Yet barking hound dogs, fireworks, screaming kids went on all night and the stuff that was jam packed into the park was insane. It looked like an episode of Hoarders. The place was filthy and unkept. Showers are so gross for being pay showers. $1.25 for 5 minutes should warrant being campground clean at least. The grounds we nasty and the smell was gross.   We have stayed now at 5 places and head to San Diego tomorrow. At this point we all want to get back to the Midwest. Just a heads up, if you really want to experience a true camping experience with fun, relaxation and amazing camp hosts and staff go to Florida and Alabama. We are a modern homeschooling family that travels very often, we are easily pleased with simple things and landscapes but this has been a dreadful experience. 

We have camped so far in Lake Tahoe at Camp Richardson, Yosemite Pines in Groveland, Mazda Seca Laguna Raceway, Seacliff, Carpinteria Beach and head to Los Rancho Cochas in San Diego tomorrow. We don't stay in the site in the daytime as we have visited San Fran, Yosemite, Lake Tahoe, LA, Hollywood, Ventura, santa Monica etc.....
Best beach in the area! By far. This one and Santa clause, the next beach over are by far the best out of Santa Barbara, Goleta, Montecito, Isla Vista and surrounding areas.

aside from their over priced-no room-too close together- camp spots the camp host and rangers are way too up tight. they discriminate against latinos and they have what might as well be kids with badges run around telling everyone to shut up.. because its one min past quiet time. my family has camped there for over 30 years and its starting to become a place where we can no longer get together with family, relax and have a good time. we can find a much nicer camp ground with friendly workers and for a much MUCH better price. with that said: if  youre prude, boring, and HATE to have fun on vacations, then carpinteria is the place to be...

A family member was camping here this weekend... I visited him on Saturday afternoon. There were no visitor parking spots available, so I parked in one of the 70 empty camping spots until something opened up.. So here come Barney  the guy in the green park uniform.. He tells me you have to move your car.. ??? I asked where do I park? The place was less than half full, was someone really going to need my parking spot?  If your looking for a pleasant spot to hang out go somewhere else.. The place is run by a few BOZOs rude volunteers and crazy want to be cops guys in green uniforms.  Oh I must say the beach was nice..

This is the safest, cleanest and most swim friendly beach I've ever been to. We have been coming here since 1997 and still as wonderful as ever. 

Here's a tip: You will get the natural tar from the beach on our feet so make sure to have some goo gone on hand as it can be sticky and you don't want to track that into your car or rental while you stay here.

Crazy and Cruel Camp Host

We checked in and the camp host harassed us at our spot about how our friends should park once they arrived. All kinds of imaginary lines were imaginarily drawn on the asphalt. When said friends arrived, Crazy and Cruel Camp Host returned and instructed them how to park. They complied, but somehow not down to the inch of his imaginary line. These are cars, mind you, not RVs. He re-instructed our friends, who were busy with their crying baby, then returned THREE minutes later, while their baby was still crying: "So you wanna move your car? You guys are starting off really bad here. You wanna CITATION?" For being an inch off an imaginary line only he can see in his head? While they are trying to soothe a baby, NOT  drinking beers and ignoring him? He actually got off his golf cart and walked over to us, intimidatingly (as intimidating as a guy with a gut who dyes his sloppy and stringy shoulder-length-hair can be). He finally to the multiple attack dogs he'd locked in his car by his RV. Way to ruin a camping experience, Crazy and Cruel Camp Host. Carpenteria, surely you can find a more mellow, a kinder camp host? Maybe the attack dogs are overkill, too?
